claims.claim_text: The 2019 Capital One cyber incident affected roughly 100 million people in the U.S. and 6 million in Canada.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: Capital One's incident page gives the breach scope, but capitalone.com is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Capital One intrusion occurred through a misconfigured web application firewall.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The DOJ press release directly states the misconfigured WAF detail, but justice.gov is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module attributes the breach blast radius to an overly permissive IAM role on the WAF with S3 access.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: However, the blast radius was massive specifically because the IAM role assigned to the WAF had overly permissive S3 access.
claims.suggested_rewrite: However, overly broad IAM permissions can greatly increase the blast radius of a cloud intrusion.
claims.rationale: The role-specific attribution is too precise for an uncited teaching anecdote; keep the least-privilege lesson and drop the unsupported implementation detail.

claims.claim_text: The module claims a specific SCP or Kubernetes admission control measure would have stopped the Capital One attack outright.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: An AWS Service Control Policy (SCP) limiting cross-account access, or a Kubernetes admission controller restricting access to the cloud metadata service, could have stopped the attack in its tracks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Guardrails such as tighter IAM policies and metadata-service protections can reduce the impact of SSRF-style cloud attacks.
claims.rationale: This is an unsupported counterfactual and overstates certainty about what would have prevented a real breach.

claims.claim_text: The Capital One breach led to an $80 million regulatory fine.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The OCC notice directly supports the fine amount, but occ.gov is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Capital One breach was later resolved with a $190 million class-action settlement fund.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The settlement notice states the $190 million fund, but the settlement host is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: AWS SCPs do not grant permissions, do not apply to the management account, and participate in hierarchical allow/deny evaluation.
claims.span_hint: section: AWS Service Control Policies (SCPs), paragraphs after example JSON
claims.rationale: AWS Organizations documentation directly covers each of these SCP behaviors.

claims.claim_text: Azure Policy supports effects such as Deny, Audit, DeployIfNotExists, Modify, Disabled, and DenyAction.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Policy, effects table
claims.rationale: Microsoft Learn explicitly lists the supported Azure Policy effects.

claims.claim_text: Azure Policy for Kubernetes deploys constraint templates and constraints to clusters and uses Gatekeeper components.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Policy, paragraph after effects table
claims.rationale: Microsoft Learn states that Azure Policy for Kubernetes deploys constraints and Gatekeeper-related components on cluster installation.

claims.claim_text: GCP custom organization policy constraints are defined with CEL conditions.
claims.span_hint: section: GCP Organization Policies, paragraph after example YAML
claims.rationale: Google Cloud's custom-constraints guide directly states that custom constraints use Common Expression Language (CEL).

claims.claim_text: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that can validate, mutate, generate, and verify image signatures.
claims.span_hint: section: Kyverno
claims.rationale: Kyverno's upstream repository README lists these capabilities directly.

claims.claim_text: Gatekeeper provides constraints, constraint templates, mutation support, audit functionality, and external data support.
claims.span_hint: section: OPA Gatekeeper
claims.rationale: Gatekeeper's upstream repository README enumerates these platform features.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table says Kyverno's CNCF status is Incubating.
claims.span_hint: section: Kubernetes Policy Engines: Kyverno vs OPA Gatekeeper, comparison table
claims.rationale: This row is stale as of March 16, 2026, when CNCF moved Kyverno to Graduated status.

claims.claim_text: The module says SCPs launched with a 2,048-character limit and that AWS raised it to 5,120 in 2019.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.anchor_text: AWS SCPs were originally strictly limited to a mere 2,048 characters when they were first launched in 2017. Enterprise customers consistently hit this severe limit so frequently that AWS officially increased it to 5,120 characters in 2019. Even at 5,120 characters, complex organizations regularly collide with the ceiling and must strategically split single logical policies across multiple SCP documents.
claims.suggested_rewrite: AWS Organizations currently limits an SCP document to 5,120 characters, so large organizations often split broad guardrails across multiple SCPs.
claims.rationale: Current AWS docs support the 5,120-character limit, but the module's precise historical timeline is not supported on an allowlisted source.

claims.claim_text: The module says OPA evaluates over 10 billion policy decisions per day globally.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2
claims.anchor_text: OPA (Open Policy Agent) systematically evaluates over 10 billion policy decisions per day across its massive production deployments globally. The Rego language was engineered specifically because existing, simpler policy languages could not adequately express the complex cross-resource relationships absolutely required for modern, cloud-scale authorization.
claims.suggested_rewrite: OPA is used at large scale in production, and Rego is a purpose-built declarative language for expressing policy over structured data.
claims.rationale: The exact throughput statistic is unsupported on an allowlisted primary source, but the broader lesson about OPA and Rego can be preserved without the number.

claims.claim_text: The module cites a 2024 CNCF survey saying 58% of organizations running Kubernetes policy engines chose Kyverno because they already knew YAML.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.anchor_text: Kyverno was initially created by Nirmata specifically because OPA Gatekeeper's complex Rego language represented a formidable barrier to adoption for standard Kubernetes platform teams. In a comprehensive 2024 CNCF survey, 58% of organizations running Kubernetes policy engines chose Kyverno, explicitly citing "we already know YAML" as their primary operational motivation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kyverno was originally created by Nirmata, and its Kubernetes-native YAML approach has made it attractive to teams that prefer not to adopt a separate policy language.
claims.rationale: The quoted survey statistic and motivation need a source; the broader adoption rationale can be retained without unsupported numbers or quotes.

claims.claim_text: HashiCorp launched Sentinel as a policy-as-code framework in 2017.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.rationale: HashiCorp's announcement gives the release date and explicitly describes Sentinel as a policy-as-code framework.

claims.claim_text: SELinux was released to the open source community in 2000 and integrated into the upstream Linux kernel in 2003.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.rationale: Red Hat's SELinux overview directly provides both dates.

claims.claim_text: AWS allows up to five SCPs per OU.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'SCP character limit workarounds using wildcards'
claims.rationale: AWS General Reference directly lists the quota of five SCPs per organizational unit.

claims.claim_text: The module says a public Kubernetes LoadBalancer can bypass cloud firewall rules entirely.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Over-relying on cloud policies, ignoring K8s'
claims.anchor_text: A public LoadBalancer can bypass cloud firewall rules entirely.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cloud-account guardrails do not replace Kubernetes-native controls for Service exposure, so you still need in-cluster policies for public LoadBalancers.
claims.rationale: The absolute 'bypass entirely' wording overclaims; the safer lesson is that cloud-account guardrails do not replace Kubernetes service-exposure controls.

further_reading.title: AWS Service Control Policies
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for SCP semantics, hierarchy, and management-account limitations.

further_reading.title: Azure Policy for Kubernetes
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how Azure Policy integrates with Kubernetes and Gatekeeper for cluster governance.

further_reading.title: Create Custom Constraints for Google Cloud Organization Policy
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary guide for CEL-based custom organization constraints in Google Cloud.

notes: Excluded quiz answers, hands-on exercise steps, Mermaid diagrams, and code blocks per pipeline rules. Table rows were only flagged when they carried hard factual, numeric, or vendor-capability claims.
